lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying saw blades for hand and powered saws, nonpowered handtools, edge tools, and related professional or industrial tools to external distributors, retailers, OEMs, construction suppliers, industrial users, and private-label customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Power-driven handtool manufacturing, metal-cutting machine tools and dies, captive internal tool plants, pure importers or distributors, non-control financing vehicles, and owner-dependent artisan toolmakers without transferable production and customer relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product and replenishment sales through distributors, retailers, OEM/private-label programs, and industrial accounts, priced per tool, blade, pack, lot, or program; saw blades and edge tools have more consumable replacement than durable handtools.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation structure for all NAICS 332 fabricated-metal manufacturing, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 332216. In May 2023, production occupations were 58.31% of NAICS 332 employment, transportation/material moving 6.51%, assemblers/fabricators 10.84%, CNC operators/programmers 5.17%, and inspectors 3.26%. I assign little language-model substitution to forging, stamping, machining, grinding, heat treatment, coating, assembly, maintenance, materials, packing, and physical testing, while assigning higher exposure to product specifications, costing, demand planning, sourcing, catalog and distributor data, quality records, sales support, and administration. Tool makers may carry more product, merchandising, and channel-management labor than the broader sector, so the proxy may understate exposure.
a.rationale: Physical production and performance testing dominate, but recurring SKUs and customer programs generate structured product-data, forecast, procurement, quotation, inventory, claims, quality, and channel-support workflows suited to assisted automation.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study across 15 independent saw-blade and handtool plants, segmented by consumable blades, industrial tools, retail tools, and private label and documenting existing automation, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for all fabricated metal manufacturing, not 332216.
No source measures current AI deployment or task exposure in this industry.

rho.rationale: Specification and catalog management, assisted costing, demand and inventory planning, sourcing review, distributor data handling, test-record triage, claims classification, and customer support are feasible within five years. Legacy systems, SKU and channel complexity, performance and safety liability, retailer-specific data, small-firm resources, and physical testing constrain implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after deployments reporting sustained labor hours, product-data errors, quote cycle time, forecast error, stockouts, claim handling, quality escapes, model exceptions, and user/customer acceptance would materially update the range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed five-year implementation rate.
Safety evidence concerns tool use and guarding, not AI adoption in manufacturing.

e.rationale: Many independent manufacturers repeatedly supply distributors, industrial accounts, and private-label programs, but branded consumer companies, import-heavy firms, captive operations, firms outside the true EBITDA band, and businesses whose channel or technical relationships are inseparable from the owner reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 198 dataset-estimated candidates covering ownership, domestic manufacturing content, repeat external revenue, branded versus OEM/private-label mix, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and owner dependence would replace this estimate.
caveats: The supplied n uses receipts and a machinery-industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
The code mixes consumable saw blades with longer-lived handtools and edge tools.

s5.rationale: A mature private tool-manufacturing base should generate retirement and strategic consolidation, but brand and channel entanglement, import dependence, technical diligence, product liability, family succession, and closures or asset-only sales reduce completed qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and industrial-tool advisers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan data for eligible firms, would materially change the probability.
caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Brand licensing, distributor acquisitions, asset sales, and internal reorganizations must be excluded unless operating control transfers.

q.rationale: Blade performance, tool durability, certifications, proprietary geometry, private-label integration, service levels, and distributor relationships can protect savings. Retail and distributor line reviews, competitive imports, private-label bids, transparent SKU comparisons, rebates, and customer cost-down demands pass part of recurring benefit through.
q.change_evidence: Distributor and retailer agreements, private-label bids, rebate and price-protection terms, line-review cadence, customer concentration, quote histories, and gross-margin bridges after automation would establish actual retention.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
Specialized industrial blades may have more pricing power than commodity handtools.

d5.bridge: BLS projects construction laborer and helper employment to grow 7% from 2024 to 2034; construction is one downstream user of handtools and saw blades, not a measure of 332216 real demand. I translate roughly half of that decade growth plus steady industrial maintenance and replacement into modest five-year demand growth. The proxy can overstate demand because tools are durable, productivity can reduce tool intensity, and imports capture consumption, or understate it because blades are consumables and industrial, forestry, maintenance, and DIY uses are omitted.
d5.rationale: Construction, repair, maintenance, fabrication, woodworking, forestry, and industrial operations continue to require cutting and hand tools. Saw blades and edge tools replenish through wear, while durable handtools have slower replacement; imports, battery-powered alternatives, economic cycles, and tool longevity constrain domestic-manufacturer demand.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census shipments and unit volumes by blade and tool category, distributor sell-through, industrial MRO consumption, construction and manufacturing activity, import data, and customer program forecasts would materially change the range.
caveats: Construction employment is an indirect downstream proxy, not tool consumption.
Domestic production can diverge from US end demand because of imports and reshoring.

o.rationale: Remaining demand is for physical cutting edges and handtools and requires accountable material selection, forming, heat treatment, grinding, coating, assembly, performance testing, packaging, and release. Software cannot supply the tool; customer direct imports, insourcing, powered substitutes, or alternative processes can replace the domestic operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer and distributor interviews plus lost-order data quantifying direct imports, powered-tool substitution, automated process replacement, tool-as-a-service or refurbishment, private-label direct sourcing, and customer insourcing would materially change this value.
caveats: Domestic operators can be displaced while US tool consumption persists.
Elimination of a task belongs in d5; o addresses who supplies tools for remaining work.

narrative.executive_view: Saw blades and handtools combine durable physical end use and consumable replacement with a bounded AI-addressable product, planning, channel, quality, and administrative layer. The industry is import- and buyer-exposed, and most labor remains tied to physical production and testing.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can manage product and distributor data, assist specifications and costing, forecast demand, plan inventory and sourcing, summarize tests and claims, draft catalog content, and support customers. Forming, machining, heat treatment, grinding, coating, assembly, inspection, performance testing, packing, and final release remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Performance, durability, specialized geometry, private-label integration, service, and distributor relationships can protect gains. Retail line reviews, bids, rebates, import comparisons, and transparent commodity SKUs share away a substantial portion over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied dataset estimates 198 firms in the target band, but actual eligibility requires verifying domestic manufacturing, repeat external accounts, normalized earnings, brand versus OEM/private-label mix, channel concentration, and owner dependence. Consumable and durable products have different economics but similar supplier channels.
narrative.demand_durability: Construction, maintenance, repair, fabrication, forestry, and woodworking still require physical blades and handtools; worn cutting products replenish repeatedly. Durable-tool longevity, imports, automation of end-user tasks, powered substitutes, and cyclicality temper growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main unknowns are six-digit labor mix, imported versus domestic content, product and channel mix, current workflow automation, distributor concentration, safety and product-liability exposure, and true LMM margins. The supplied inputs mix vintages and use a machinery margin bridge.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat blade replacement and distributor programs generate structured specification, forecast, inventory, quality, and channel workflows around essential physical products.
narrative.principal_weakness: Physical production limits AI substitution, while imports, retail line reviews, and transparent commodity comparisons constrain benefit retention.

sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: BLS reports production occupations at 839,220 workers and 58.31% of NAICS 332 employment in May 2023, transportation/material moving at 6.51%, assemblers/fabricators at 10.84%, CNC workers at 5.17%, and inspectors at 3.26%.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 332216 Saw Blade and Handtool Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines the industry as manufacturing saw blades of all types, including blades for powered saws, and nonpowered handtools and edge tools.

sources.title: Hand and Power Tools - Overview
sources.supports: OSHA states that hand and power tools are common across nearly every industry, enable otherwise difficult or impossible tasks, and can cause severe injuries if used or maintained improperly.

sources.title: 1926.300 - General Requirements for Tools
sources.supports: OSHA requires employer- or employee-furnished hand and power tools to be maintained in safe condition and requires guards for power-operated tools designed to accommodate them, supporting operator and safety accountability.

sources.title: Construction Laborers and Helpers
sources.supports: BLS projects construction laborer and helper employment to grow 7% from 2024 to 2034; used only as an indirect downstream activity proxy for part of tool demand.
